1.matchText: Olmec heartland cities had been abandoned
1.blurb: By 100 BCE the great Olmec centers at San Lorenzo, La Venta, and Tres Zapotes had been abandoned for centuries, their colossal heads left staring into the jungle. But the Olmec legacy lived on in the calendar systems, religious iconography, and urban planning principles that Teotihuacan would inherit and transform. The Olmec invented the template; Teotihuacan built the metropolis.

While Teotihuacan was coalescing from refugee villages into a planned city, the Chavín culture in the Andes had already collapsed and regional kingdoms were emerging in Peru. Both hemispheres were experiencing the same pattern: an early unifying culture fragments, and something larger rises from the pieces. But the two experiments unfolded in complete isolation from each other.

1.matchText: Roman Republic transitioning into the Roman Empire
1.blurb: Around the same decades that Teotihuacan's grid was being laid out in the Valley of Mexico, Augustus was transforming the Roman Republic into the Roman Empire. Two of history's most ambitious urban experiments were taking shape simultaneously on opposite sides of the Atlantic, separated by 6,000 miles of open ocean and utterly unaware of each other.
Teotihuacan rose in the highland vacuum after the Olmec — inheriting the Mesoamerican template and scaling it into the New World's first true metropolis.
Teotihuacan and Monte Albán were the two great highland capitals of Classic Mesoamerica — rivals and trade partners, each a planned ceremonial city.

2.matchText: Olmec had used caves for ritual
2.blurb: Cave ritual was deeply embedded in Mesoamerican religion from the Olmec onward. At Oxtotitlan and Juxtlahuaca, Olmec-era cave paintings depict rulers and deities in underground spaces associated with earth, water, and the underworld. Teotihuacan's Pyramid of the Sun built over a real cave was the most literal version of this ancient tradition: a real cave, a real mountain, and a real temple at the top.

2.matchText: Rome under Trajan was the capital of an empire
2.blurb: Around 100 CE, when the Pyramid of the Sun was being completed, Trajan's Rome was the capital of an empire of 60 million people with concrete domes, aqueducts, and professional legions. Teotihuacan had none of these technologies but built a city plan equally ambitious. The two cities peaked in population at the same time, among the largest on earth, knowing nothing of each other's existence.
The Street of the Dead organizes the city around blood-and-cosmos ritual the Olmec first systematized.

3.blurb: The Olmec carved feathered serpent imagery on monuments at La Venta over a thousand years before Teotihuacan built its spectacular temple. The Feathered Serpent deity — later Quetzalcoatl to the Aztecs, Kukulkan to the Maya — was one of the most enduring religious concepts in the Americas, transmitted from Olmec origins through Teotihuacan to civilizations that outlasted both.

4.matchText: Bronze Age in the Old World
4.blurb: In the Old World, the Bronze Age economy depended on controlling copper and tin sources — whoever held the mines held the power. Teotihuacan's obsidian monopoly was the Mesoamerican equivalent: a single essential cutting material that every community needed, controlled by one city. Both systems concentrated wealth and political power in the hands of whoever commanded the supply chain.

5.matchText: the Olmec civilization a thousand years earlier
5.blurb: The Gulf Coast residents of the Merchants' Barrio came from the same tropical lowlands that had produced the Olmec civilization a thousand years earlier. The Olmec heartland's obsidian-poor landscape had made it dependent on highland trade networks — a dependency that Teotihuacan now exploited by housing Gulf Coast traders permanently inside the city.

5.matchText: Western Empire would formally end in 476 CE
5.blurb: Teotihuacan's collapse around 550 CE came barely 75 years after the formal end of the Western Roman Empire in 476 CE. Two of the ancient world's most impressive urban experiments — one in the Old World, one in the New — fell within a century of each other, each leaving behind a power vacuum that successor states spent centuries trying to fill.

6.blurb: The Entrada of 378 CE was a defining moment for both civilizations. From Teotihuacan's perspective, it was the most dramatic projection of power in the city's history. From the Maya perspective, it launched Tikal's rise to superpower status in the Classic Period — the Teotihuacan-backed dynasty used its foreign prestige to dominate the Maya lowlands for over a century.

6.matchText: Roman Empire was in the process of splitting
6.blurb: In 378 CE, the same year as the Entrada, the Roman Empire suffered one of its worst military disasters at the Battle of Adrianople, where Gothic cavalry destroyed a Roman army and killed Emperor Valens. Two great civilizations on opposite sides of the Atlantic were both at inflection points — Rome fragmenting, Teotihuacan projecting power at maximum range.

6.matchText: Moche culture on Peru's north coast
6.blurb: While Teotihuacan was projecting power into the Maya lowlands, the Moche on Peru's north coast were producing their finest portrait vessels and building the Huacas del Sol and de la Luna. Both civilizations peaked around 400 CE, dominated their respective regions through a mix of trade and military power, and collapsed within a century of each other — the Americas' twin experiments in pre-Columbian urbanism.
The 'Entrada' — Teotihuacan's intervention at Tikal in 378 CE — is the moment the metropolis reached into the Maya world and rewrote its dynasties.

7.blurb: The Storm God and Feathered Serpent of Teotihuacan became Tlaloc and Quetzalcoatl — the Aztecs literally worshipped at, and mythologized, this ruined city.

8.blurb: While the Mesoamerican Epiclassic was filling the vacuum Teotihuacan left behind, the Andes were running an almost exact contemporary version of the same story — Tiwanaku and Wari rising into the political space that the Moche-Nazca regional kingdoms had vacated, in a Middle Horizon (600-1000 CE) that overlaps almost perfectly with the Epiclassic. Two hemispheres, no contact, the same structural moment.

After Teotihuacan's collapse around 550 CE, the Maya lowlands erupted in escalating warfare between Tikal and Calakmul — the two superpowers that dominated the Late Classic Period. Whether the removal of Teotihuacan's stabilizing influence freed the Maya city-states to fight more aggressively is debated, but the chronological coincidence is striking.

8.matchText: continued for another century or so after
8.blurb: Monte Albán's decline after Teotihuacan's collapse ended one of the longest diplomatic relationships in Mesoamerican history. The two cities had maintained the Oaxacan Barrio connection for centuries — Zapotec residents living permanently in Teotihuacan, maintaining their culture while participating in the great city's economy. When both cities fell, the entire diplomatic architecture of Mesoamerica dissolved.
Teotihuacan's fiery fall sent shockwaves through the Maya world it had dominated — the superpower's collapse reordering Classic Mesoamerica.
Centuries later the Aztecs named the ruins 'the place where the gods were made' — Teotihuacan as the sacred origin of the world they built.
